content.text: Azure Portal navigation is built on blades — full-width panels that slide in from the right as you drill deeper into resources. A typical click path stacks four or five blades: sidebar service → resource list → resource overview → settings sub-blade → child resource. The breadcrumb trail in the top bar reflects this stack. Understanding blades and navigation patterns prevents the common confusion of losing your place or opening multiple browser tabs unnecessarily.

attrs.alt: Azure Portal blade stack: sidebar leads to resource group list (Blade 1), rg-prod detail (Blade 2), vnet-prod resource (Blade 3), and Subnets child blade (Blade 4) with breadcrumb trail across top

content.text: A blade is a full-height panel in the portal's main area. It has three zones: a command bar at the top with context-sensitive actions (Add, Delete, Refresh, Export), a left-hand settings navigation for resources, and a content area that fills the remainder. When you click an item in the left navigation or a list, a new blade slides in from the right, stacking on top of the previous one.
Hub blade: the top-level service blade (e.g., 'Virtual machines') showing all resources of that type
Resource blade: the overview for a single resource (e.g., a specific VM named vm-prod-01)
Settings sub-blade: a child blade opened from the resource's left-hand settings menu (e.g., Networking, Disks, Monitoring)
Child resource blade: a blade for a nested resource such as a NIC, a subnet, or a diagnostic setting

Blade width is fixed at approximately 700px for most blades. You can scroll horizontally when many blades are stacked. Use the breadcrumb to jump back rather than clicking the browser's Back button, which re-loads the page.

The breadcrumb bar at the top of the main area always shows your full path from Home to the current blade. Each segment is a clickable link that jumps directly back to that level without re-loading unrelated blades.

# Example breadcrumb path:
Home > Resource groups > rg-prod > vnet-prod > Subnets

# Clicking "rg-prod" jumps back to the rg-prod blade
# and closes the blades for vnet-prod and Subnets

# Clicking "Resource groups" jumps back to the
# full resource groups list blade

# Keyboard equivalent: press Alt+Left Arrow to go back one blade
# (same as clicking the left-pointing breadcrumb arrow)

When you open a resource blade, a left-hand vertical menu appears inside that blade listing every settings category for that resource type. This menu is the primary way to move between settings without stacking new blades for each category.
Essentials, status, metrics summary, recent activity, quick actions
ARM operations on this resource (who changed what and when)
Role assignments, check access, deny assignments, classic admins
Tag key-value pairs; applies to the resource directly
Guided troubleshooter specific to the resource type
Metrics charts, Alerts, Diagnostic settings, Log Analytics
ReadOnly or Delete locks on this resource

# The left menu is scrollable when there are many sections.
# Use Ctrl+F in most browsers to find text in the visible page,
# including the left menu items.

# Common left-menu destinations and their typical position:
Networking    → under Settings (3-4 clicks down)
Access control (IAM) → near the top of most resources
Diagnostic settings  → under Monitoring section
Locks                → near the bottom

# To quickly open the Access control blade via portal URL:
https://portal.azure.com/#@<tenant>/resource/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/<rg>/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/<vm>/accessControl

Not all clicks open a full blade. Many actions open a context pane — a narrower panel (around 400px) that slides in alongside the current blade without replacing it. Context panes are used for quick edits, confirmations, and property viewers that do not require the full blade treatment.
Clicking a resource name in a list
Clicking 'Add tag' on the Tags blade
Clicking a settings menu item (Networking, IAM)
Clicking an alert rule to view details
Clicking a notification in the bell menu
Opening Resources in a New Browser Tab

By default, clicking a resource opens it in the same blade stack. To open it in a new tab (useful when comparing two resources), right-click the resource name and choose 'Open link in new tab', or middle-click the link. Each tab maintains its own independent blade stack.

# Open a resource directly in a new tab:
1. Right-click the resource name link
2. "Open link in new tab"

# Or: copy the resource's direct URL
1. Navigate to the resource in the portal
2. Copy the URL from the browser address bar
3. Paste into a new tab — the URL encodes the full navigation path

# Example resource URL (each resource has a permanent link):
https://portal.azure.com/#@contoso.onmicrosoft.com/resource/subscriptions/
00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000/resourceGroups/rg-prod/providers/
Microsoft.Network/virtualNetworks/vnet-prod/overview

# Bookmark this URL to return directly without navigating the sidebar

Resource list blades (resource groups, virtual machines, storage accounts) include a filter bar that searches by name, type, location, or tag within the current blade scope. This is separate from the global search in the top bar.

# Filter options inside a resource group blade:
Filter by name     → partial match, case-insensitive
Filter by type     → e.g., "Virtual machine", "Storage account"
Filter by location → e.g., "East US", "West Europe"
Filter by tag      → Key=Value (e.g., Environment=Production)
Filter by status   → Running / Stopped / Deallocated

# Combine filters with AND logic — each active filter narrows the list
# Clear all filters: click the X next to each filter chip, or "Clear all filters"

# Column sorting: click any column header to sort ascending;
# click again for descending; third click clears the sort
Resources visible in the current blade only
Resources by name, type, tag, location
Filters the visible list in real time
Navigating directly to a known resource
Narrowing a large list within a service or RG

The portal home page shows recently visited resources and services. The left sidebar shows starred Favourite services. These two mechanisms speed up repeat access to frequently used resources without searching.

# Recent resources appear on the home page under "Recent":
- Up to 20 recently opened resources are shown
- Click any to jump directly to its blade
- Hover over a recent resource to see its full name and resource group

# The same list is accessible via:
Home > Recent resources (link at bottom of the recents section)

# To clear recent resources:
Settings (gear) > Portal settings > Appearance + startup views >
Recents > "Clear recents"

# Add a service to Favourites:
1. Click "All services" in the sidebar
2. Type to search (e.g., "Key vaults")
3. Click the star (☆) — it turns solid (★) and the service appears in Favourites

# Remove from Favourites:
1. Click the solid star (★) next to the service in All Services
   OR right-click the service in the sidebar > "Remove from Favorites"

# Reorder Favourites:
Settings (gear) > Portal settings > Appearance + startup views >
drag the Favourites list to reorder

# Default favourites (pre-starred for new accounts):
- Virtual machines
- App Services
- SQL databases
- Storage accounts
- Azure Active Directory

When your account has access to multiple Azure AD tenants or multiple subscriptions, the portal shows resources from the active directory and selected subscriptions. You must switch context before the target resources become visible.

# Switch between directories (tenants):
1. Click your account avatar in the top-right corner
2. Click "Switch directory"
3. Select the target directory from the list
4. The portal reloads fully in the new directory context

# Filter which subscriptions are visible:
1. Click the filter icon in the top bar (next to the notifications bell)
2. Under "Subscriptions", check/uncheck individual subscriptions
3. Click "Apply" — the portal immediately shows only selected subscriptions

# Quick shortcut to subscription filter:
https://portal.azure.com/#view/Microsoft_Azure_Billing/SubscriptionsBlade

# List all tenants your account can access
az account list --query "[].{Name:name, Sub:id, Tenant:tenantId}" -o table

# Switch to a different subscription
az account set --subscription "sub-production"

# Switch to a different tenant (requires login)
az login --tenant "contoso.onmicrosoft.com"

# Show current context
az account show -o table
Every interactive element in the portal is keyboard-accessible. Learning the most common shortcuts significantly speeds up resource navigation.
Move between interactive elements in a blade
Activate the focused link or button
Close the current context pane or dialog
Show the full keyboard shortcut reference panel
The Access control (IAM) blade is one of the most frequently visited settings blades in the portal. Understanding its sub-tabs prevents confusion when assigning or auditing role assignments.
Summary counts of role assignments, deny assignments, and classic admins at this scope
List all current role assignments; filter by role, user, or principal type
View Azure-managed or Blueprints-created deny assignments (read-only)
Enter a user, group, service principal, or managed identity to see their effective permissions at this scope
Browse all built-in Azure roles and their permission definitions
Legacy Account Admin, Service Admin, Co-Admin assignments (deprecated)
Finding Effective Permissions via the Portal

# Use "Check access" to audit a user's effective permissions:
1. Open a resource, resource group, or subscription blade
2. Click "Access control (IAM)" in the left menu
3. Click the "Check access" tab
4. In "Find" > type a user, group, or app name
5. Select the principal from the dropdown
6. The portal shows all role assignments inherited and direct at this scope

# Via CLI — same information:
az role assignment list \
  --assignee jane.smith@contoso.com \
  --scope /subscriptions/<sub-id>/resourceGroups/rg-prod \
  --include-inherited \
  -o table
Assigning a Role via the IAM Blade

# Portal workflow:
# IAM blade > + Add > Add role assignment
# Tab 1 (Role): select "Contributor"
# Tab 2 (Members): search and select the user or group
# Tab 3 (Conditions): optional attribute-based conditions (ABAC)
# Review + assign

# CLI equivalent:
az role assignment create \
  --role "Contributor" \
  --assignee "jane.smith@contoso.com" \
  --scope "/subscriptions/<sub-id>/resourceGroups/rg-prod"

# Assign to a service principal (e.g., a managed identity):
IDENTITY_ID=$(az identity show \
  --name mi-myapp \
  --resource-group rg-prod \
  --query principalId -o tsv)

az role assignment create \
  --role "Storage Blob Data Reader" \
  --assignee-object-id "$IDENTITY_ID" \
  --assignee-principal-type ServicePrincipal \
  --scope "/subscriptions/<sub-id>/resourceGroups/rg-prod/providers/Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/stprod001"

Resource locks are applied from the Locks blade in any resource or resource group. A lock prevents accidental deletion or modification. Understanding the locks blade prevents confusion when delete operations fail unexpectedly.
DELETE operations on the resource or its children
Any write or delete (PUT, PATCH, DELETE)

# Apply a Delete lock via CLI
az lock create \
  --name lock-no-delete-rg-prod \
  --lock-type CanNotDelete \
  --resource-group rg-prod

# Apply a ReadOnly lock on a specific resource
az lock create \
  --name lock-readonly-vnet \
  --lock-type ReadOnly \
  --resource-group rg-prod \
  --resource-name vnet-prod \
  --resource-type Microsoft.Network/virtualNetworks

# List locks on a resource group (includes inherited locks)
az lock list --resource-group rg-prod -o table

# Delete a lock (must have Owner or User Access Administrator role)
az lock delete \
  --name lock-no-delete-rg-prod \
  --resource-group rg-prod

ReadOnly locks on storage accounts prevent listing keys, which breaks many SDK operations. ReadOnly locks on App Service plans prevent scaling. Test locks in a non-production environment before applying to production resources.

The Diagnostic settings blade (under Monitoring in most resource left menus) configures where Azure sends platform logs and metrics for a resource. This is the portal entry point for connecting resources to Log Analytics, Event Hubs, and storage.

# Enable diagnostic settings via CLI (portal equivalent)
LAW_ID=$(az monitor log-analytics workspace show \
  --resource-group rg-monitoring \
  --workspace-name law-prod \
  --query id -o tsv)

# Send VM platform metrics and logs to Log Analytics
az monitor diagnostic-settings create \
  --name diag-vm-prod-01 \
  --resource "/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/rg-prod/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/vm-prod-01" \
  --workspace "$LAW_ID" \
  --metrics '[{"category":"AllMetrics","enabled":true}]' \
  --logs '[]'

# Enable for a storage account (blob service sub-resource)
az monitor diagnostic-settings create \
  --name diag-storage-blob \
  --resource "/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/rg-prod/providers/Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/stprod001/blobServices/default" \
  --workspace "$LAW_ID" \
  --logs '[{"category":"StorageRead","enabled":true},{"category":"StorageWrite","enabled":true},{"category":"StorageDelete","enabled":true}]' \
  --metrics '[{"category":"Transaction","enabled":true}]'

# List current diagnostic settings for a resource
az monitor diagnostic-settings list \
  --resource "/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/rg-prod/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/vm-prod-01" \
  -o table

The Activity Log blade (available on every resource, resource group, and subscription) shows the history of ARM management plane operations. It is the first place to check when a resource changes unexpectedly.

# View activity log in portal:
# Resource blade > Activity log (in left menu)
# Filter by: Timespan, Subscription, Resource group, Resource, Operation, Status, Initiated by

# CLI equivalent — more powerful for scripting
az monitor activity-log list \
  --resource-group rg-prod \
  --start-time 2024-06-01T00:00:00Z \
  --end-time 2024-06-30T23:59:59Z \
  -o table

# Find all Failed operations in the last 7 days
az monitor activity-log list \
  --resource-group rg-prod \
  --status Failed \
  --max-events 100 \
  --query "[].{Time:eventTimestamp, Operation:operationName.localizedValue, Status:status.localizedValue, Caller:caller}" \
  -o table

# Find who deleted a resource
az monitor activity-log list \
  --resource-group rg-prod \
  --start-time 2024-06-01T00:00:00Z \
  --query "[?contains(operationName.value,'delete')].{Time:eventTimestamp, Op:operationName.value, By:caller}" \
  -o table
The Tags blade on any resource or resource group lets you add, edit, and delete key-value tag pairs directly in the portal without deploying a template. Changes take effect immediately.

# View current tags
az tag list \
  --resource-id "/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/rg-prod/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/vm-prod-01" \
  -o table

# Add or update a tag (merges with existing tags)
az resource tag \
  --resource-group rg-prod \
  --name vm-prod-01 \
  --resource-type Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines \
  --tags Environment=Production CostCentre=CC-001 Owner=alice@contoso.com

# Remove a specific tag (replace all with the ones you want to keep)
az resource tag \
  --resource-group rg-prod \
  --name vm-prod-01 \
  --resource-type Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines \
  --tags Environment=Production CostCentre=CC-001

# Tag a resource group (tags on RG do not auto-propagate to resources)
az group update \
  --name rg-prod \
  --tags Environment=Production Project=Contoso2024
Navigating Locks, Tags, and IAM from the CLI

All portal navigation paths have a direct CLI or REST equivalent. When automating onboarding of new resources, the following script applies standard governance settings that would otherwise require several clicks through multiple blades.

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# apply-governance.sh — run after creating a new resource group

RG_NAME="rg-new-project"
LOCATION="eastus"
OWNER_EMAIL="alice@contoso.com"
LAW_ID="/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/rg-monitoring/providers/Microsoft.OperationalInsights/workspaces/law-prod"
ENV="Production"
COST_CENTRE="CC-042"

# 1. Apply standard tags to the resource group
az group update \
  --name "$RG_NAME" \
  --tags Environment="$ENV" CostCentre="$COST_CENTRE" Owner="$OWNER_EMAIL" ManagedBy=Terraform

# 2. Assign Owner role to the project lead
az role assignment create \
  --role Owner \
  --assignee "$OWNER_EMAIL" \
  --scope "/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/$RG_NAME"

# 3. Assign Reader role to the ops monitoring group
OPS_GROUP_ID=$(az ad group show --group "ops-readonly" --query id -o tsv)
az role assignment create \
  --role Reader \
  --assignee-object-id "$OPS_GROUP_ID" \
  --assignee-principal-type Group \
  --scope "/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/$RG_NAME"

# 4. Apply a delete lock to prevent accidental deletion
az lock create \
  --name lock-no-delete \
  --lock-type CanNotDelete \
  --resource-group "$RG_NAME"

echo "Governance applied to $RG_NAME: tags, RBAC, and delete lock"

Azure Portal navigation is a blade-based system where each click stacks a new panel to the right. The breadcrumb at the top tracks the full path and lets you jump back to any ancestor without reloading. Resource blades have a left-hand settings menu for moving between settings categories within the same resource. Context panes open for lightweight edits; full blades open for deeper navigation. Use the global search (Ctrl+/) for direct navigation to known resources, and the blade-scoped filter bar to narrow large lists. Switching tenants or subscription filters in the top bar controls which resources are visible across the entire portal session.
